title: The Shape of the Thing
body:
### Where we are

Telesis has been built deterministic-harness-first: scaffold, context generation,
drift detection, review pipeline, milestone validation. These are the tools that
keep a project honest. They work, and they're valuable — but they're the skeleton,
not the organism.

The original vision was always a multi-agent system: self-driving, self-correcting,
capable of navigating the full development process and producing professional-quality
artifacts (documentation, working code aligned with designs, tested and correct). The
deterministic harness is the foundation that system stands on — you can't have
self-correction without something to correct against.

### The AI-first development problem

Observations from building in an AI-first/AI-only environment:

**The speed asymmetry.** Coding assistants produce code far faster than a human can
review it. The human becomes the bottleneck — not at writing, but at verifying.
Automated code review helps, but reviewing diffs in isolation misses the bigger
question: is this implementation aligned with the project's design and goals?

**The guidance gap.** AI assistants frequently need input, guidance, or correction to
navigate through development tasks. They make locally reasonable decisions that are
globally wrong — correct code that doesn't match the architecture, working features
that drift from requirements, clean implementations that miss the point.

Most of these failure modes can be addressed by an orchestrator (quarterback) with a
handful of specialized agents (teammates). The orchestrator holds the project context
and intent; the agents do focused work within that frame. When an agent drifts, the
orchestrator catches it — not after the PR, but during the work.

**The memory problem.** Context resets between sessions. Every new conversation starts
from scratch, re-reading the same docs, re-discovering the same constraints. The human
carries continuity in their head and re-injects it manually. This doesn't scale. The
project needs its own memory — not just what was decided (ADRs) or what was built (code),
but what was tried, what failed, why things are the way they are.

### The review inversion (and its limits)

Current review flow: CI runs review → findings posted to GitHub → human triages on
GitHub → telesis imports triage decisions back. This creates tight platform coupling
and a sync problem (CI's `.telesis/` is ephemeral).

The proposed inversion: all triage happens via telesis CLI → `.telesis/` is the source
of truth → platforms are notification targets, not data sources.

**The gap:** This only works cleanly when review runs locally. CI review exists because
it's the "second pair of eyes" after the development session — you push and walk away,
CI reviews the full PR diff. CI's `.telesis/` is ephemeral, so findings generated there
can't round-trip through local state without either:

1. Git as sync — CI commits findings back to the branch (ugly but simple)
2. Accept the asymmetry — CI review and local review are different contexts with
   different trade-offs, don't force-unify them
3. External state service — distributed platform complexity, not where we want to go

No conclusion yet. The current artifact-based cache (upload/download review sessions)
is a partial solution but only syncs between CI runs, not CI-to-local.

### Event-driven architecture

Current telesis: sequential CLI commands that read/write files. Each command is
independent — run, produce output, exit.

Emerging idea: an event-driven backbone where agents/services observe events they care
about and respond. The review pipeline emits "findings generated," the triage system
observes and prompts for action, the chronicler observes development events and updates
project memory, the drift checker observes file changes and flags violations.

This could be:
- A long-running daemon with a real event bus
- A lightweight event log (`.telesis/events.jsonl`) processed on each invocation
- Something like RxJS/signals as the runtime backbone
- The orchestrator explicitly directing traffic between agents

The key shift: from "human runs commands" to "system reacts to development events."
The orchestrator becomes the quarterback — it holds the project context, assigns work
to specialized agents, and catches drift in real-time rather than after the fact.

### What the memory layer looks like

Telesis already has pieces of a memory system:
- Notes (`.telesis/notes.jsonl`) — developer observations
- Dismissals (`.telesis/dismissals.jsonl`) — review triage decisions
- Review sessions (`.telesis/reviews/`) — review history and findings
- Telemetry (`.telesis/telemetry.jsonl`) — model call logs
- Living docs (`docs/`) — architecture, requirements, decisions

What's missing:
- **Exploration memory** — "we considered X, Y felt promising, Z is a dead end"
  (this journal is a manual version of that)
- **Process memory** — "last time we changed the adapter layer, we broke the
  feedback loop because..." (the chronicler's job)
- **Intent memory** — "the human wants the review to stop re-raising FilterStats
  location after they've triaged it three times" (dismissals are a primitive version)
- **Session continuity** — context that persists across coding sessions without
  the human manually re-injecting it

The chronicler (v0.11.0) was envisioned as batch extraction from transcripts, but
maybe it's better as a real-time observer that builds project memory as development
happens. The difference: batch processing looks backward; an observer builds memory
forward, in the moment.

### The quarterback model

The core insight: most AI development failures are coordination failures, not
capability failures. The coding assistant can write good code. The reviewer can find
real issues. The drift checker catches real violations. But nobody is making sure
they're all pulling in the same direction, and nobody remembers what happened last time.

The orchestrator's job:
- Hold the project's intent (spec, architecture, constraints)
- Assign work to specialized agents within that frame
- Monitor agent output for alignment with intent (not just correctness)
- Catch drift during work, not after the PR
- Build and maintain project memory as a byproduct
- Surface the right context to the right agent at the right time

This is what telesis becomes. The CLI commands we've built are the agent capabilities.
The orchestrator is the missing piece that ties them together.

### Open questions

1. Is CI review worth the sync complexity, or should review move fully into the
   development session (local-first, pre-push)?
2. What's the minimum viable orchestrator? Is it a daemon, a CLI wrapper, an MCP
   server, or something else?
3. How does the event-driven model interact with the current file-based state?
   Is `.telesis/events.jsonl` enough, or does this need a real runtime?
4. Can the journal concept (this file) evolve into a telesis-managed artifact
   (`telesis journal`) that feeds into context generation?
5. What are the specific agent roles? Reviewer, drift checker, chronicler — what
   else? A "requirements alignment" agent? A "documentation freshness" agent?
6. How does the orchestrator interact with the human? Is it a conversation partner
   (like Claude Code), a background service that interrupts when needed, or a
   dashboard?

title: Architecture Direction and CI Decision
body:
### Monolithic binary with in-process events

The orchestrator will be a single binary, not a distributed system. In-process event
streams (Node EventEmitter, lightweight observables, or similar) provide the reactive
backbone. Agents are functions within the same runtime that subscribe to an event bus,
not separate processes communicating over a network.

This avoids the entire distributed systems tax: no servers, no ports, no message
brokers, no infrastructure. When the orchestrator runs, it boots, loads `.telesis/`
state, agents react to events within the process lifetime, and state is persisted on
exit. JSONL remains the durable persistence format; the event bus is ephemeral and
in-memory.

Concern noted: JSONL works for append-only logs read by one process at a time (current
usage), but would break down as an event bus with concurrent readers/writers. The
in-process event stream sidesteps this. JSONL is for persistence, not coordination.

### Dropping CI review

**Decision: Remove the telesis CI review workflow.**

Rationale:
- CI review is a *reaction* to code that's already been pushed. By the time findings
  land in a GitHub thread, the development session is over and the context is lost.
- The quarterback vision makes CI review redundant — if the orchestrator catches drift
  during work, review happens in the loop, not after it.
- CI review is the source of the sync problem, the GitHub coupling, and most of the
  review pipeline complexity we've been wrestling with.
- GitHub holds zero importance to this project beyond code hosting and eventual sharing.
  GitHub Actions is ceremony for the sake of ceremony in this context.
- "Works on my machine" is not a real problem that requires CI to solve here.

Options for the transition:
- Reinstate bop in CI as a proven, zero-maintenance safety net (if any CI review wanted)
- Or simply skip CI review entirely and run `telesis review` locally when desired
- Either way, the review pipeline focus shifts from "CI-compatible" to "useful within
  the orchestrator's development loop"

### Impact on backlog

Issues #50-58 are review pipeline polish (feedback loop, sync, GitHub integration).
With CI review going away:
- #58 (summary findings feedback loop) — irrelevant if no CI review
- #56 (extract sync-replies from CLI) — irrelevant
- #51 (dismiss reply to GitHub) — irrelevant
- #55, #57 (architecture cleanup) — low priority, address when touched
- #50, #52, #53 — local review UX, still relevant but lower priority
- #36 (streaming telemetry) — still relevant for large projects

Energy redirects to: orchestrator design, memory system, agent architecture.

title: Orchestrator Shape, Events, and Agent Roster
body:
### Daemon + TUI + Notifications

The orchestrator will be a **daemon** — a long-running background process with OS-level
supervision (LaunchAgent on macOS, systemd on Linux).

The TUI is a **client** that connects to the daemon over a local socket. It can
detach/reattach without losing state. Multiple clients are possible (TUI, CLI commands
that need daemon state, eventually MCP). The daemon runs headless when no client is
attached.

OS-level notifications (macOS `terminal-notifier` or `osascript`, native Node libs) for
unattended alerts: "drift detected," "milestone gate: 3/5 criteria met."

OpenClaw's gateway is prior art for this pattern: single process, WebSocket control plane,
hot reload for config changes, two-stage agent completion (accepted → streaming → done).
The gateway multiplexes control/RPC + HTTP + UI hooks on a single port. Telesis could
expose the same port for TUI control, CLI queries, and eventually MCP.

### Event model: RxJS

**Decision: Use RxJS for the in-process event backbone.**

Rationale over EventEmitter:
- Backpressure/composition: `debounce`, `throttle`, `buffer`, `switchMap` are essential
  for a daemon processing filesystem events, agent results, and user commands concurrently
- Observable lifecycle: subscriptions clean up properly; EventEmitter listener leaks are
  a classic footgun in long-running processes
- Stream transformation: agents observing compound conditions ("file changed AND drift
  check passed AND no active review") compose naturally with `combineLatest`/`withLatestFrom`
- Team familiarity: prior RxJS experience

Rationale over Signals (Preact signals, TC39 proposal):
- Signals are optimized for UI reactivity, not event-stream processing
- Conceptually similar to RxJS but less mature for daemon workloads

Event format — discriminated union:
```typescript
interface TelesisEvent {
  readonly type: string;        // "file.changed", "drift.detected", "review.complete"
  readonly timestamp: number;
  readonly source: string;      // which agent/watcher emitted it
  readonly payload: unknown;    // type-narrowed by discriminant
}
```

Key discipline: **events are facts** ("this happened"), **commands are directives** ("do
this"). Events flow from agents/watchers to the bus. Commands flow from the orchestrator
to agents. The orchestrator observes events and decides what commands to issue.

The hardest part of eventing is standardizing event formats so consumers are pluggable —
this is correct. The transport is almost interchangeable if the contracts are clean.

### Agent roster: evolved from VISION.md

The original six agents in VISION.md, revisited against what we've built and learned:

| Agent | Core Responsibility | Foundation | Notes |
|-------|-------------------|-----------|-------|
| **Reviewer** | Code quality, correctness, alignment | `telesis review` (v0.5-v0.10) | Fully realized. Battle-tested. |
| **Architect** | Structural integrity, drift, ADR enforcement | `telesis drift` (v0.3) | Reframed: drift + LLM semantic layer. Detects shape violations, circular deps, ADR contradictions. Overlaps resolved with Reviewer: Reviewer owns code quality, Architect owns structural intent. |
| **Spec Keeper** | Spec↔implementation alignment, spec evolution | None | Sharper role: not just detecting divergence (that's drift) but proposing spec updates when implementation reveals new requirements. "The code added retry logic not in the spec. Update spec or remove retry?" |
| **Milestone Tracker** | Progress validation, gate control | `telesis milestone` (v0.9) | Deterministic base exists. LLM layer adds judgment: "criteria #3 says 'handles edge cases' — does it?" |
| **Chronicler** | Provenance trail, decision memory | `telesis note` (v0.4) | The memory system. Records decisions, rationale, rejected paths. Distinct from documentation maintenance. |
| **Test Sentinel** | Test validity, coverage meaning | None | Hardest agent to build well. Needs spec context to know what tests should validate. Catches mock divergence, implementation-detail assertions, meaningless coverage. |

**New agents identified:**

| Agent | Core Responsibility | Foundation | Notes |
|-------|-------------------|-----------|-------|
| **Scribe** | Documentation currency | `telesis context` (v0.1) | Distinct from Chronicler: Scribe maintains docs (CLAUDE.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, PRD.md), Chronicler maintains the provenance trail. `telesis context` on autopilot but smarter. |
| **Planner** | Work breakdown, sequencing | Human-authored plans | Looks at acceptance criteria + codebase + architecture, proposes build sequences. Closes the loop between "what to build" and "how to build it." |

**MVP orchestrator agents (minimum viable swarm):**
1. Reviewer (already built)
2. Architect (drift + semantic layer)
3. Chronicler (memory system)

The others come online incrementally as the orchestrator matures.

### `telesis journal` as managed artifact

The journal feeds naturally into the Chronicler's domain. It is the human-authored
version of what the Chronicler will eventually maintain automatically.

As a first-class command:
- Versioned and tracked in `.telesis/`
- Feeds into context generation (agents can read it)
- Chronicler can eventually append based on observed development events
- Serves as the thinking layer between "messy conversation" and "crystallized ADR"

### Open questions (narrowed)

1. ~~Is CI review worth the sync complexity?~~ **No. Dropped.**
2. ~~What's the minimum viable orchestrator?~~ **Daemon with local socket.**
3. ~~What event model?~~ **RxJS.**
4. How does the TUI connect to the daemon? WebSocket? Unix socket? IPC?
5. What does the MVP daemon actually *do* before agents are wired in? Filesystem
   watching + event logging? Is that useful enough to justify the daemon infrastructure?
6. What's the Chronicler's input? Development events from the daemon? Git hooks?
   Transcript analysis? All of the above?
7. How does the Planner interact with the human? Does it propose plans for approval,
   or does it execute autonomously within milestone boundaries?

title: OpenClaw Ecosystem Analysis
body:
### The question

Should telesis exist independently, layer on top of OpenClaw, or is it reinventing
something that already exists?

### What OpenClaw is

The OpenClaw ecosystem has three distinct architectural layers:

1. **OpenClaw Gateway** — a messaging router (chat platforms → AI agents). Long-running
   daemon, WebSocket control plane, OS-level supervision (LaunchAgent/systemd), ACP
   protocol for IDE integration. It is a *conversational platform*, not a development tool.

2. **Symphony (caclawphony)** — a development orchestrator that polls Linear for issues,
   creates isolated per-issue workspaces, and dispatches coding agents (Codex) with
   bounded concurrency. GenServer manages poll loop, dispatch, retry with exponential
   backoff, and process monitoring. Status dashboard renders ANSI terminal output. This is
   the closest analog to what telesis wants to become.

3. **Lobster** — a pipeline composition engine. Unix pipes but typed and resumable.
   `command1 | approve --prompt "Continue?" | command2`. Approval gates halt the pipeline
   and return resume tokens. Clean SDK: `new Lobster().pipe(exec('...')).pipe(approve({...})).run()`.

4. **ClawPort** — Next.js web dashboard for monitoring OpenClaw agents. SSE streaming for
   live logs, REST API routes that shell out to `openclaw` CLI, cost tracking, kanban,
   memory browser. Not a TUI.

5. **acpx** — headless CLI client for Agent Client Protocol sessions. JSONL event logs
   with segment rotation, session persistence, JSON-RPC over stdio.

### Symphony vs Telesis — the key comparison

| Aspect | Symphony | Telesis |
|--------|----------|---------|
| What it orchestrates | Single coding agent per issue | Multiple specialized agents per concern |
| Work source | External issue tracker (Linear) | Internal — project's own spec, milestones, drift |
| Agent model | One general-purpose agent with massive prompt | Panel of specialized agents with distinct roles |
| Intelligence | All intelligence in the prompt template | Distributed across agents + deterministic checks |
| Memory | None — each run starts fresh | First-class — project memory persists across sessions |
| Workspace | Per-issue directory copies | The actual project repo |
| Human interaction | Gate states in Linear | TUI + OS notifications for interactive collaboration |
| Orchestration | Poll → dispatch → wait → repeat | Event-driven — observe → react → coordinate |

Symphony is a *batch job runner* for issues. Telesis is a *continuous development
companion* that holds context and reacts to development events. You wouldn't layer
telesis on top of Symphony because Symphony's coordination model (poll Linear → dispatch
agent → wait for completion) doesn't match telesis's needs.

### Verdict: Telesis justifies its existence

**Different problem domain.** OpenClaw routes conversations. Symphony dispatches agents
to issues. Telesis holds project intent, detects drift, orchestrates specialized agents,
and maintains memory across the project lifecycle.

**Different agent model.** OpenClaw treats agents as general-purpose assistants given a
prompt. Telesis treats agents as specialized roles with distinct concerns, tools, and
coordinated oversight.

**Different memory model.** OpenClaw has no project memory. Symphony starts fresh each run.
Telesis's value proposition is persistent context — across sessions, PRs, milestones.

**Different interaction model.** OpenClaw is conversational (chat in, response out).
Telesis is a development companion (observe events, react when needed, pause for human
judgment at gates).

### What to borrow from OpenClaw

**Adopt:**
- Daemon lifecycle management (install/start/stop/status, LaunchAgent/systemd)
- Versioned agent policy file pattern (Symphony's `WORKFLOW.md` → telesis agent configs)
- Lobster's approval/resume pattern for milestone gates
- StatusDashboard's terminal rendering approach for TUI
- acpx's session persistence model (JSONL event logs with segment rotation)
- Symphony's bounded concurrency with backoff retry

**Do not adopt:**
- Messaging/routing architecture (wrong domain)
- Poll-dispatch-wait orchestration loop (telesis needs event-driven, not polling)
- Single-agent-per-issue model (telesis needs multi-agent-per-concern)
- Linear/issue-tracker coupling (telesis's work source is the project itself)
- Web dashboard (stay in the terminal)

### Observations from Symphony's WORKFLOW.md

Symphony's most interesting design choice is putting the entire agent policy — including
the prompt, gate definitions, workspace hooks, and concurrency settings — in a single
versioned file (WORKFLOW.md with YAML front matter + markdown body). The prompt template
uses Mustache variables for issue context.

For telesis, this suggests each agent could have a policy file:
```
.telesis/agents/
  reviewer.md      # review prompt + config (personas, thresholds)
  architect.md     # drift rules + structural intent checks
  chronicler.md    # memory extraction patterns + journal config
```

These would replace the hardcoded prompts in `src/agent/review/prompts/` and make agent
behavior user-configurable. The prompt becomes a contract between the human and the agent,
versioned with the project.

title: The Full Loop: Telesis as Work Executor
body:
### The missing piece in the vision

The previous entries describe telesis as the *governance layer* — the control system that
watches, validates, and corrects. But the end game is larger: telesis is also the thing
that **dispatches coding agents to do work**.

The full loop:
```
Intake → Understand → Plan → Dispatch → Monitor → Validate → Correct → Complete
```

### How this changes the picture

The specialized agents (Reviewer, Architect, Chronicler...) aren't just watchers. They're
the **support team** around a coding agent that telesis dispatches:

1. **Intake** — work arrives via TUI input, issue tracker feed (Jira, GitHub Issues,
   Linear), or a lightweight interview (evolved from `telesis init`)
2. **Understand** — the orchestrator uses project context (spec, architecture, ADRs,
   memory, prior attempts) to understand what the work requires
3. **Plan** — the Planner breaks the work into steps, identifies dependencies, sequences
   the build (or defers to the human for approval)
4. **Dispatch** — the Dispatcher selects a coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini — agent-
   agnostic), constructs a rich context package, sets up the workspace (worktree, branch),
   and launches the agent
5. **Monitor** — while the agent works, specialist agents watch: Architect checks for
   drift, Reviewer examines output, Milestone Tracker validates progress
6. **Validate** — when the agent completes, deterministic checks run (drift, tests, lint),
   and the Reviewer does a final pass
7. **Correct** — if something's wrong, the orchestrator feeds corrections, additional
   context, or halts for human input
8. **Complete** — the Chronicler records what happened, the Scribe updates docs, the loop
   closes

### Why context management had to come first

The scaffold, context generation, drift detection, review pipeline, milestone validation —
these aren't just CLI tools. They're the **agent capabilities** of the specialized
oversight agents. When the orchestrator dispatches a coding agent:
- `telesis drift` becomes what the Architect runs continuously
- `telesis review` becomes what the Reviewer runs against the agent's output
- `telesis milestone check` becomes what the Milestone Tracker uses to validate progress
- `telesis context` becomes what the Scribe uses to keep docs current

The CLI was always the prototype for the agent layer.

### What makes this better than Symphony

Symphony dispatches coding agents too, but:
- **Context injection** — Symphony gives a prompt template with issue details. Telesis
  gives *everything it knows about the project*: spec, architecture decisions, what was
  tried before, conventions, dismissal history, the journal.
- **Active oversight** — Symphony fires and forgets (dispatch, wait, check result).
  Telesis has specialized agents watching *during* the work.
- **Agent-agnostic** — Symphony is coupled to Codex. Telesis treats the coding agent as a
  pluggable execution layer. The value is in the context and oversight, not the agent.
- **Memory across runs** — Symphony's agent starts fresh. Telesis's agent starts with
  "last time you worked on this module, here's what happened."

### The Dispatcher role

A new agent role emerges: **The Dispatcher** (or Foreman). Its job:
- Takes a unit of work + project context
- Selects the appropriate coding agent
- Constructs the context package (relevant docs, code, constraints, prior attempts)
- Sets up the workspace (worktree, branch, `.telesis/` config)
- Launches the agent with the right prompt and tool configuration
- Monitors the execution lifecycle (started, streaming, completed, failed)

This is distinct from the Planner (strategic sequencing) and the orchestrator (overall
coordination). The Dispatcher knows *how to talk to coding agents* — prompt construction,
tool configuration, protocol adaptation.

### Revised agent roster

| Agent | Role | Foundation |
|-------|------|-----------|
| **Reviewer** | Code quality, correctness, alignment | `telesis review` |
| **Architect** | Structural integrity, drift, ADR enforcement | `telesis drift` |
| **Spec Keeper** | Spec↔implementation alignment, spec evolution | — |
| **Milestone Tracker** | Progress validation, gate control | `telesis milestone` |
| **Chronicler** | Provenance trail, decision memory | `telesis note` |
| **Scribe** | Documentation currency | `telesis context` |
| **Test Sentinel** | Test validity, coverage meaning | — |
| **Planner** | Work breakdown, sequencing | Human-authored plans |
| **Dispatcher** | Agent selection, context packaging, workspace setup, execution lifecycle | — |

### Work intake patterns

Three intake channels, all converging on the same orchestration loop:

1. **Human via TUI** — "I want to add retry logic to the API client." The orchestrator
   may run a lightweight interview to clarify intent, check against the spec, then plan
   and dispatch.

2. **Issue tracker feed** — Jira/GitHub/Linear issues in a "ready" state get pulled in.
   The orchestrator enriches them with project context (which Symphony can't do because
   it has no project memory) before dispatching.

3. **Internal triggers** — drift detection finds a violation, milestone check finds
   unmet criteria, the Reviewer flags a pattern. These generate internal work items
   that the orchestrator can auto-dispatch (within autonomy boundaries) or surface
   to the human for approval.

### Open questions

1. ~~What protocol does the Dispatcher use?~~ **ACP. See next entry.**
2. How much autonomy does the orchestrator have? **Configurable. See next entry.**
3. Worktree/branch strategy for concurrent dispatches? **Default low concurrency
   (1-2). Judgment call per-project.**
4. ~~How does the human observe/intervene?~~ **ACP event stream + TUI. See next entry.**

title: ACP as the Dispatcher Protocol
body:
### Decision: ACP (Agent Client Protocol) for coding agent communication

ACP is to coding agents what LSP is to language servers. It standardizes the protocol
so the Dispatcher doesn't need per-agent integration code.

**Why ACP over MCP or direct subprocess:**
- ACP is purpose-built for orchestrator↔coding-agent communication
- JSON-RPC over stdio (same transport as MCP/LSP)
- Session lifecycle: new, load, close, cancel — with crash recovery
- Structured output: tool calls, diffs, thinking (not ANSI scraping)
- Cooperative cancel (`session/cancel` → agent gracefully stops)
- Queue-based prompt submission (submit while busy, prompts drain in order)
- Multi-turn persistent sessions that survive across invocations

**Agent support (all via ACP, either native or adapter):**

| Agent | ACP Support | Adapter |
|-------|------------|---------|
| Claude Code | Adapter | `claude-agent-acp` (Zed Industries) |
| Codex | Adapter | `codex-acp` (Zed Industries) |
| Gemini | Native | `gemini --acp` |
| Cursor | Native | `cursor-agent acp` |
| GitHub Copilot | Native | `copilot --acp --stdio` |
| Pi | Adapter | `pi-acp` |
| Kiro | Native | `kiro-cli acp` |
| Qwen | Native | `qwen --acp` |

MCP goes the other direction — it's for agents to *use tools*, not for orchestrators to
*drive agents*. Some coding agents can act as MCP servers, but ACP is the fit-for-purpose
protocol for the Dispatcher's job.

### acpx as the integration layer

`acpx` (openclaw/acpx) is a headless CLI client for ACP that already solves the hard
problems:
- Agent adapter registry with auto-download (`npx`)
- Persistent sessions scoped to git roots
- Named parallel sessions (`-s backend`, `-s frontend`)
- NDJSON event output with stable envelope (sessionId, requestId, seq, type)
- Queue-based prompt submission with IPC
- Crash reconnect (dead process → respawn → session/load → resume)
- Permission controls (`--approve-all`, `--approve-reads`, `--deny-all`)

Integration options for telesis:
1. **Use `@agentclientprotocol/sdk` directly** — tightest integration, full control
2. **Use acpx as a library** — `npm install acpx`, call its session management
3. **Shell out to acpx** — subprocess, simplest MVP, good enough to start

### Live event streaming and TUI attachment

ACP's structured event stream solves the observation problem:

1. Daemon spawns coding agent via ACP, subscribes to NDJSON event stream
2. Events flow through the internal RxJS bus — specialist agents observe them
3. TUI connects to daemon, requests event stream for a session
4. Human sees: thinking, tool calls, diffs, completions — in real time
5. Human can intervene: `session/cancel` for cooperative stop, then redirect

The Architect observing the same event stream can detect drift *as tool calls happen*
— "the agent just edited src/github/client.ts, which violates the adapter boundary
in ADR-001" — before the agent finishes its turn.

### Autonomy configuration

```yaml
# .telesis/config.yml
orchestrator:
  autonomy: supervised    # supervised | autonomous | gated
  max_concurrent_agents: 2
  workspace_strategy: worktree  # worktree | copy
  default_agent: claude         # claude | codex | gemini | ...
```

- **supervised** — propose work and wait for human approval before dispatching
- **autonomous** — dispatch within milestone boundaries, surface results
- **gated** — autonomous but pause at defined gate states for human judgment

Default: supervised. Low concurrency (1-2) is the right starting point — merge
conflicts, resource contention, and cognitive overhead of tracking multiple concurrent
agents argue against high parallelism.

### Remaining open questions

1. How does the Dispatcher construct the ACP prompt? Does it inject telesis context
   as part of the prompt text, or does it configure the agent's CLAUDE.md / system
   instructions via the workspace setup?
2. Should the Dispatcher use acpx sessions directly, or should it manage its own
   session layer with ACP SDK underneath?
3. How do specialist agents (Reviewer, Architect) observe ACP events? Do they
   subscribe to the RxJS bus, or do they run as separate ACP-aware processes?
4. What's the workspace teardown policy? Keep worktrees for review? Auto-clean
   after merge? Configurable retention?

title: v0.18.0 — Future Enhancement Opportunities
body: v0.18.0 shipped the full loop pipeline with simplest-possible implementations. Deferred expansion opportunities: LLM-generated commit messages (use ModelClient for contextual messages from diff + plan), daemon-driven orchestration (reactive event handlers that auto-trigger pipeline stages), git worktree isolation (concurrent plan execution), smart branch naming (LLM-suggested or configurable patterns), PR body generation (LLM-generated descriptions), issue metadata sync (labels/assignees/milestones back to GitHub), conflict resolution assistance (LLM-assisted rebase/merge), post-commit review integration (auto-run telesis review before push), pipeline resumability (detect partial state and resume), webhook-triggered intake (auto-import new issues via GitHub webhooks).

title: Dispatch show: reconstruct agent narrative
body: telesis dispatch show currently displays agent output events as bare sequence numbers (seq=1, seq=2, ...), which is not useful for understanding what the agent actually did. The raw ACP events in .telesis/sessions/ contain token-level streaming text that can be concatenated into a readable narrative. A --verbose or --text flag on dispatch show should reconstruct and display the full agent output as readable text, rather than 966 lines of seq=N. This would make session replay genuinely useful for post-hoc review of what an agent did and why.

title: Quality gates: from named slots to arbitrary gate chains
body: The v0.18.x quality gate implementation uses named slots (format, lint, test, build, drift, review) with configurable command strings. This works for the common case across languages — swap 'pnpm run lint' for 'bundle exec rubocop' or 'go vet ./...'. But it assumes every project fits the same slot taxonomy. Real projects have custom gates: deploy previews, license scans, migration checks, compliance steps, security scans. The natural evolution is an ordered list of arbitrary gates, each with a name, command, and behavior flags (e.g., amendOnChange for formatters, blockOnFailure vs warnOnFailure). This also opens the door to gate plugins — a gate could be a telesis command, a shell script, or an MCP tool call. Defer until cross-project usage (v1.0) surfaces real needs that named slots can't handle.

title: Claude Code as a daemon client
body:
### The fourth client

The daemon's client model has three established slots: TUI (interactive terminal), CLI
(one-shot commands that query daemon state), and eventually web (ClawPort-style dashboard).
There's a natural fourth: AI coding assistants like Claude Code.

### The MCP angle

The mechanism is MCP (Model Context Protocol). Telesis exposes an MCP server, Claude Code
connects to it as a client. This inverts the current relationship — instead of Claude Code
being *told* about telesis via a static CLAUDE.md file, it *connects* to telesis as a live
service.

What Claude Code could access via MCP:
- **Live project context** — current milestone, active findings, convergence state, drift
  status. Not a snapshot from `telesis context`, but real-time state.
- **Review operations** — trigger `telesis review` on staged changes, read findings, dismiss
  findings, check convergence — all within the conversation.
- **Drift queries** — "does my change violate any architectural constraints?" before committing.
- **Pipeline control** — start a pipeline run, check status, approve gates.
- **Memory** — query the journal, notes, dismissal history. Claude Code's own memory system
  (MEMORY.md) is per-session; telesis's memory is per-project and persistent.

### Why this matters

The current workflow: human runs `telesis review`, reads output, tells Claude Code what to
fix. With MCP integration, Claude Code could run `telesis review` itself, read the findings,
fix them, re-review, and iterate — the convergence loop happens within the AI session
without human intermediation for mechanical steps.

The daemon becomes the bridge: it holds the live state, the MCP server exposes it, and any
client (TUI, CLI, web, Claude Code) can connect and interact with the same coherent project
state.

### Relationship to the quarterback model

This is the quarterback model from a different angle. The journal entries on orchestrator
architecture imagine telesis dispatching coding agents via ACP. The MCP angle is the
reverse: the coding agent (Claude Code) reaches *into* telesis for context and oversight.
Both directions are valuable — ACP for telesis-initiated work, MCP for agent-initiated
queries. The daemon serves both.

### Open questions

1. What's the MCP server surface area? Start minimal (read-only project state) or include
   mutation operations (dismiss findings, trigger reviews)?
2. How does authentication work for the MCP server? Local-only (Unix socket) is simplest.
3. Does the MCP server require the daemon to be running, or can it work standalone?
4. How does this interact with Claude Code's existing CLAUDE.md consumption? Complementary
   (MCP for live state, CLAUDE.md for static conventions) or replacement?

title: Polyglot Support: Audit & Path Forward
body:
Audited the entire codebase for TypeScript-specific and single-language assumptions to scope polyglot support.

## Architecture: Mostly Ready

The framework is already language-aware in the right places:
- Review, dispatch, and oversight prompts all receive `primaryLanguage` and use it for context
- Templates (architecture, vision, PRD, ADR, TDD) are language-agnostic
- Quality gates are user-configurable shell commands
- Agent context flows language info through the entire stack

## Implementation: TypeScript-Coupled in 3 Subsystems

### 1. File Scanning (`src/drift/scan.ts`)
`findTypeScriptFiles()` hardcodes `.ts` extension. Every drift check that scans source files is TypeScript-only by inheritance. Fix: generalize to `findSourceFiles(extensions: string[])` with language-to-extension mapping.

### 2. Drift Check Registry (`src/drift/checks/index.ts`)
7 of 15 checks are TypeScript-specific (sdk-import, commander-import, no-process-exit, test-colocation, command-registration, rxjs-import, acpx-import). The registry is static — no mechanism to enable/disable checks per language. Fix: add `applicableLanguages` metadata to each check, filter at runtime.

### 3. Milestone Checks (`src/milestones/check.ts`)
Hardcodes `pnpm test`, `pnpm run build`, `pnpm run lint`. Fix: pull from quality gates config or auto-detect package manager.

## Config Schema Change

`project.language` is a single string. Needs to become `project.languages: string[]` (or keep `language` as primary with optional `additionalLanguages`). The interview extraction also coerces to single string.

## Minimal Viable Polyglot Support

1. Config: `languages: string[]` with backward compat for `language: string`
2. Drift: Language metadata on checks + runtime filter
3. File scanning: Extension-configurable scanner
4. Milestone checks: Use quality gates config instead of hardcoded pnpm
5. Everything else already works — prompts, templates, agents all pass language as context

The key insight: this is a focused refactoring of 3 subsystems, not a broad rewrite. The architecture was designed well enough that language is already contextual info throughout the agent stack.

title: Milestone Prioritization: MCP Connector Before Polyglot
body:
Evaluated three candidate milestones for post-v0.19 work: polyglot support, daemon hardening, and an MCP connector to the daemon. Decision: daemon hardening (if needed) → MCP connector → polyglot support.

## Reasoning

**MCP connector is highest leverage.** It transforms Telesis from a CLI you switch to into ambient project intelligence available inside Claude Code sessions. Claude Code already supports MCP servers — exposing the daemon as an MCP server means any coding session can query project state, review findings, plan status, dispatch agents, and access the event stream without leaving the conversation. This is the closest step toward the vision of Telesis as an operating layer between humans and agents.

**Polyglot is important but not blocking.** The TypeScript coupling is real (7/15 drift checks are TS-specific, milestone checks hardcode pnpm, file scanning only handles .ts) but workaround-able. Review, dispatch, planning, and oversight already work for any language — they pass language as context. A Go or Python project could use most of Telesis today by configuring quality gates and skipping TS-specific drift checks. The polyglot fix is a bounded cleanup (~3 subsystems + config schema), not an architectural change. Value only materializes when actually onboarding a non-TypeScript project.

**Daemon hardening may be a prerequisite.** The MCP connector needs a solid daemon underneath — reliable connections, event streaming, state queries. If the daemon is still immature in any of those areas, hardening comes first.

## Priority Language Targets (for eventual polyglot milestone)

1. Go — highest priority, frequent use
2. R — data science workflows
3. Python — general purpose
4. Ruby — occasional, others would benefit

## Tracking

- Polyglot support: issue #76
- MCP connector: needs issue (create when milestone is scoped)
- Daemon hardening: evaluate current state before deciding if separate milestone needed

title: Roadmap: Polyglot → MCP → Cost Optimization → Daemon Orchestrator
body:
After extensive architectural discussion, settled on a four-phase roadmap for post-v0.19 development. This supersedes the earlier "daemon hardening → MCP → polyglot" sequence from the same day — the discussion revealed that the ordering should be inverted.

## Decision

1. **Polyglot support** — Remove TypeScript-specific assumptions (issue #76). Bounded, 2-3 day effort across 3 subsystems (drift checks, file scanning, milestone commands) + config schema change. Immediate ROI: makes telesis usable on Go, R, Python, Ruby repos.

2. **MCP server** — Expose all telesis capabilities as MCP tools. Claude Code (or any MCP client) becomes the orchestrator. This gets us most of the autonomous operation vision without building a custom orchestrator — Claude Code already has reasoning, tool calling, human-in-the-loop, and memory. The key insight: the orchestration layer doesn't need to be custom-built if an LLM client can chain tool calls.

3. **Cost optimization** — Before scaling autonomous usage, audit where tokens actually go. The review pipeline (3 personas × convergence rounds × verification) is likely the biggest driver. Look for: smarter early stopping, cheaper models for low-stakes operations (validation, noise filtering), reducing redundant context in multi-round reviews. A feature currently costs $20-25 including reviews — this needs to come down for autonomous operation to be economically viable.

4. **Daemon orchestrator** — Build daemon-as-brain only when we need capabilities Claude Code can't provide: background polling for new issues, webhook ingestion, multi-session coordination, fire-and-forget execution while offline. The daemon's role simplifies in this model — it's the background execution engine, not the reasoning layer.

## Rationale for ordering

- Polyglot first because it's bounded and immediately useful across real repos
- MCP before daemon because Claude Code already IS an orchestrator — wrapping telesis in MCP tools gives us autonomous operation "for free" without building custom orchestration
- Cost optimization before daemon because autonomous operation amplifies token spend — need to make individual operations cheaper before running them unsupervised
- Daemon last because its scope shrinks once MCP exists — it becomes background execution rather than full orchestration

## Architectural insight

The original vision was daemon-as-orchestrator with CLI/TUI/MCP as clients. The revised vision: the orchestration intelligence lives in the LLM client (Claude Code), telesis provides the capabilities (via MCP tools), and the daemon handles only what requires persistence between sessions (background jobs, polling, event streaming). This is a cleaner separation — telesis doesn't need to reimplement reasoning that the LLM client already has.

## Key concern: cost

Current per-feature cost (~$20-25) makes fully autonomous operation expensive at scale. This isn't blocking for personal use but would block team adoption or high-volume automation (e.g., processing every Jira ticket). Cost optimization is a prerequisite for the "dispatch all the shit work" use case (version bumps, linter upgrades, trivial bug fixes from Jira).

## Origin of MCP idea

MCP occurred as a natural extension of two observations: (1) telesis is almost always driven from Claude Code already, and (2) the CLI-as-daemon-client idea from early design discussions. The immediate catalyst was wanting to run telesis review against remote PRs (assigned code reviews on GitHub Enterprise repos without local telesis config) — a use case where CLI invocation doesn't work because the code isn't local. That capability is deferred but informed the MCP priority.

title: LLM Provider Abstraction: Audit & Coupling Analysis
body:
Audited the codebase for Anthropic/Claude-specific assumptions to scope provider-agnostic LLM support.

## Three layers of coupling

### 1. Naming/branding (~20 instances, trivial to fix)
- CLAUDE.md filename (38 files reference it)
- claude.md.tmpl template, claude-md-freshness drift check
- ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var
- Documentation references throughout docs/

### 2. Design coupling (~12 instances, moderate)
- ModelClient internals: message format, streaming events, cache token fields, error types
- Interview engine hardcodes "Anthropic requires messages start with user role" (line 46 of engine.ts)
- Telemetry records provider: "anthropic", tracks Anthropic-specific cache tokens
- Pricing defaults keyed to Anthropic model IDs
- ACPX adapter handles Anthropic extended thinking blocks

### 3. Hard coupling (8 instances, significant work)
- @anthropic-ai/sdk is the only provider implementation
- Retry logic uses Anthropic.APIConnectionError and Anthropic.APIError
- Streaming parses Anthropic SSE events (message_start, content_block_delta, message_delta)
- Token extraction uses Anthropic-specific field names (cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens)

## Containment is good

The SDK import rule (enforced by drift check) means only src/agent/model/client.ts imports @anthropic-ai/sdk. The rest of the codebase talks to ModelClient. This means a provider adapter pattern could be implemented behind ModelClient without touching callers.

## Path to multi-provider

1. Create src/agent/provider/ with pluggable providers (AnthropicProvider, OpenAIProvider, GoogleProvider)
2. Make agent context filename configurable (CLAUDE.md vs AGENTS.md vs custom)
3. Abstract streaming event protocol behind provider adapter
4. Make interview "start with user message" requirement provider-aware
5. Generalize telemetry to handle different token categories per provider
6. Centralize model defaults with provider prefix

## Relationship to roadmap

This is adjacent to the current roadmap (polyglot → MCP → cost → daemon). Could be tackled as part of MCP work (since MCP clients may use different providers) or as a separate milestone. Not urgent — Anthropic models are the only ones being used currently.

title: Bootstrapping telesis into existing projects
body:
telesis init currently assumes a fresh project — it creates doc stubs from templates and fails if .telesis/config.yml already exists. For established repos, the workflow needs to be different:

- The repo already has code, architecture, conventions, and possibly existing documentation
- telesis init should be able to introspect the existing codebase and generate docs that reflect reality, not empty templates
- Questions to resolve: Should there be a separate `telesis bootstrap` command? Should `telesis init` detect an existing repo and switch to bootstrap mode? How does the AI interview adapt when there's already code to analyze?
- The scaffold should handle: existing .gitignore (append, don't overwrite), existing README (preserve), existing docs/ directory (merge or skip)
- Consider: generating VISION.md and PRD.md from existing code + README is fundamentally different from generating them from an interview about a new project

title: Evaluate what in .telesis/ should NOT be gitignored
body:
Currently .gitignore excludes most .telesis/ runtime data: telemetry.jsonl, reviews/, dismissals.jsonl, sessions/, intake/, notes.jsonl, journal.jsonl. The config.yml is tracked. But there's a question about what SHOULD be committed vs what's ephemeral:

Should be committed (project-wide, shared):
- .telesis/config.yml (already tracked) — project configuration
- .telesis/pricing.yml — cost model shared across team
- .telesis/agents/*.md — oversight policy files

Probably should NOT be committed (user/machine-specific):
- telemetry.jsonl — grows indefinitely, machine-specific token usage
- sessions/, reviews/ — large, machine-specific review/dispatch history
- orchestrator.json — ephemeral state, machine-specific
- decisions/ — transient orchestrator decisions

Gray area:
- dismissals.jsonl — team-shared review triage decisions? Or machine-specific?
- journal.jsonl — design decisions feel like they should be shared
- notes.jsonl — development notes might be personal or shared
- intake/ — work items from GitHub, could be reconstructed from source
- plans/ — task plans, could be useful for team visibility

The key question: what serves the "memory is a first-class feature" principle? If journal entries and dismissals capture team knowledge, they should be committed. If they're personal scratch space, they shouldn't.

title: User guide: clarify notes vs journal vs ADR for new users
body:
The user guide doesn't clearly explain when to use each of the three knowledge capture mechanisms. This will be a stumbling point for new users. The distinction:

- **Notes** (`telesis note add`): Lightweight, tagged, ephemeral. Think sticky notes. Quick observations, reminders, debugging breadcrumbs. Tags enable filtering (e.g., all notes tagged "git" or "config"). Short-lived — you might clean these up periodically. No title, just text + tags.

- **Journal** (`telesis journal add`): Titled, dated, narrative. Think lab notebook. Design decisions in progress, investigation summaries, rationale for approaches tried and rejected. Longer-lived than notes — they document the thinking behind the work. Title + body structure encourages more thoughtful entries.

- **ADRs** (`telesis adr new`): Formal, numbered, immutable once accepted. Think architectural record. Significant decisions that affect the project's direction — technology choices, structural patterns, integration approaches. ADRs have a template (context, decision, consequences) and a lifecycle (Proposed → Accepted → Superseded). They live in docs/adr/ as markdown files, not in .telesis/.

The user guide should have a comparison table or decision tree: "If you're capturing X, use Y." Examples:
- "I noticed the config parser has a bug" → note (tagged "config", "bug")
- "We investigated three caching strategies and chose Redis because..." → journal entry
- "We're switching from REST to GraphQL for the public API" → ADR

The notes-and-journal.md page and adrs-and-tdds.md page should cross-reference each other and include this guidance prominently.

title: Correction: notes and journal descriptions for user guide
body:
Follow-up to the previous entry on notes vs journal vs ADR. Two important corrections:

**Notes are not ephemeral scratch — they're living context.** Notes are included in the generated CLAUDE.md, which means they become part of the LLM's context in every session. They are evolving, additional instructions that shape how coding agents behave. A note tagged "git" saying "SSH remote required for workflow scope" isn't a sticky note — it's a persistent instruction that every future Claude Code session will see. This makes notes significantly more important than "quick observations." They're the lightweight mechanism for steering agent behavior without editing CLAUDE.md directly.

**Journals capture reasoning, not just decisions.** Journal entries document the WHY behind choices — the investigation process, the alternatives considered, the reasoning that led to a conclusion. This is distinct from ADRs which capture the WHAT (the decision itself) in a formal structure. A journal entry might say "We explored three approaches to the orchestrator state machine and here's why we chose a deterministic FSM over an LLM agent." The corresponding ADR would just record "Decision: Use a deterministic state machine for the orchestrator."

Updated mental model:
- **Notes** = living instructions that shape every future session (in CLAUDE.md context)
- **Journal** = reasoning trail — why decisions were made, what was explored
- **ADR** = formal record of significant architectural decisions (immutable once accepted)

title: strongdm ecosystem analysis — ideas for Telesis
body:
Comprehensive analysis of strongdm's open source AI development ecosystem (2026-03-16). Five projects examined: Attractor (pipeline engine), CXDB (context store), Agate (project orchestrator), Leash (agent sandbox), AttractorBench (conformance benchmark).

Key finding: Agate is the closest direct comparison to Telesis — both are development lifecycle orchestrators. Agate is simpler (GOAL.md → agate auto) but less rigorous (no drift detection, shallow review, no enforcement). Telesis is more opinionated and thorough but harder to get started with.

12 ideas identified across the ecosystem, prioritized by leverage:

FROM AGATE (highest leverage — direct orchestrator improvements):
1. `suggest` command for real-time steering without formal decisions
2. Sprint retrospectives as automated lifecycle step (post-completion, pre-idle)
3. Auto-generated skill/persona files per project language
4. Recovery skill before human escalation (diagnose + fix environment, then retry)
5. Quick-start mode bypassing full ceremony for small tasks

FROM ATTRACTOR (pipeline engine patterns):
6. Model stylesheet for per-stage model assignment (CSS-like selectors)
7. Context fidelity modes (full/summary/fresh) for token efficiency
8. Pipeline visualization (DOT rendering of orchestrator state)

FROM CXDB (storage model concepts — adopt the ideas, not the server):
9. Turn DAG structure (parent pointers in JSONL stores for branching history)
10. Content-addressed deduplication for large payloads (.telesis/blobs/)

FROM LEASH (agent sandboxing):
11. Container sandbox integration for dispatched agents (dispatch.sandbox: leash)

FROM ATTRACTORBENCH (evaluation patterns):
12. Conformance scoring model for automated acceptance criteria validation

title: Init on existing repo: first real observations from bop bootstrap
body:
Ran telesis init on the bop repo (existing codebase). Three concrete findings:

1. INTERVIEWER SHOULD INTROSPECT THE CODEBASE. The interview asks questions as if starting from scratch. For an existing repo, the interviewer should notice there's code, explore the structure, read key files (package.json, existing README, src/ layout), and ask more guided questions based on what it finds. "I see you have a Go CLI with these commands — tell me about the design intent" is better than "What language are you using?"

2. PRESERVE EXISTING CLAUDE.md CONTENT. Many projects already have a hand-written CLAUDE.md with project-specific instructions, conventions, and context. telesis init generates a new CLAUDE.md from the interview docs, overwriting the old one. Before generating, telesis should: (a) read the existing CLAUDE.md, (b) identify content that won't be captured in the generated docs (VISION.md, PRD.md, etc.), (c) auto-create telesis notes from that orphaned content so it feeds back into the regenerated CLAUDE.md via the notes section. This preserves institutional knowledge that was already captured.

3. .mcp.json NOT CREATED BY INIT. The scaffold creates .claude/settings.json and .claude/hooks/git-preflight.sh but does not create .mcp.json pointing at telesis-mcp. This should be part of the scaffold output — if telesis-mcp is on PATH, create .mcp.json with the right config.

title: Bop experience report: first external project end-to-end
body:
First full telesis lifecycle on an external project (bop, Go, ~280 files). Ran init → notes → intake → plan → dispatch → review → ship. Key outcomes:

WHAT WORKED: Notes system intuitive. Plan generation accurate and well-scoped. Review convergence fast (1 round, 0 findings, ~$0.04). Drift detection useful as pre-commit check.

8 FRICTION POINTS IDENTIFIED:
1. CLAUDE.md overwritten without preservation (already tracked #104)
2. Generated docs hallucinated codebase structure — ARCHITECTURE.md had fabricated internal/ layout
3. MCP server not auto-configured (#105 already tracked)
4. MCP tools are read-only for intake and dispatch — no intake approve, dispatch run
5. Large MCP responses exceed token limits (76 issues = 145K chars, 323-event session = 82K chars)
6. Note tag duplication in CLAUDE.md — multi-tagged notes rendered under every tag heading
7. Drift check false positive — expected-directories flagged telesis-internal src/ paths on a Go project
8. GITHUB_TOKEN not inherited from gh auth chain

Items 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 are NEW findings not previously tracked.

title: Strategic positioning: telesis is backend infrastructure, not a CLI tool
body:
After reflecting on Claude Code's native features (hooks, skills, memory, subagents, CLAUDE.md), realized that telesis's value is NOT as a human-facing CLI. Claude has become the universal interface for development work. Telesis is the structured state layer and process engine that lives behind whatever LLM interface the developer uses.

This reframes the product:
- MCP server is the primary interface, not CLI commands
- CLI exists for debugging, scripting, and CI — not human interaction
- Skills trigger telesis workflows through natural conversation with Claude
- The orchestrator is the process engine that the LLM delegates to

What's core: structured stores, orchestrator state machine, drift detection, review pipeline, context generation, MCP server. What's demoted: CLI output polish, interactive CLI init, help text formatting. What's integration glue (replaceable per provider): skills, hooks, MCP config templates.

Estimated ~15-20% of total effort was CLI polish that's now demoted in importance. But business logic was already CLI-framework-agnostic by design (clean architecture from the start), so nearly all of it transfers directly.

title: Daemon is the provider-neutral enforcement runtime
body:
Corrected an earlier analysis that flagged the daemon as "potentially obsolete." The opposite is true — the daemon's importance grows as provider agnosticism becomes real.

Skills and hooks are Claude Code-specific mechanisms. If a developer uses Codex, Gemini, Cursor, or something that doesn't exist yet, those enforcement mechanisms vanish. The daemon is the portable layer that fills that gap:

- Preflight enforcement via git event watching (not Claude Code hooks)
- Contextual guidance via MCP resources (not provider-specific skills)
- Process nudging via push event monitoring (not hook-based gating)
- Background intelligence: drift checks, update notifications, stale milestone detection

The architecture should be: core capability in daemon + MCP server (thick, provider-neutral), with thin provider-specific adapters (Claude Code: skills + hooks; Codex: TBD; bare MCP: works with any client). Never put core enforcement logic solely in skills or hooks — implement in the daemon first, then create adapters that enhance the experience for specific providers.

title: Two-mode development: exploratory upstream vs. structured downstream
body:
Identified two distinct phases in the AI-assisted development workflow that require fundamentally different interaction models:

1. Upstream (human-driven, exploratory): Requirements iteration, design exploration, trade-off analysis, task decomposition. This is conversational and iterative. The CLI is a poor fit — it feels like filling out forms when you want to have a conversation. This phase naturally lives in dialogue with an LLM.

2. Downstream (agent-driven, structured): Intake receives well-defined discrete tasks, orchestrator drives execution through the lifecycle (plan → execute → review → complete). The state machine fits perfectly here.

Telesis currently covers downstream well. The upstream phase has no telesis support. The boundary should be intake: upstream produces preprocessed, well-defined, discrete tasks; intake receives them. Telesis should NOT try to formalize the upstream process into a state machine — that would kill the exploratory energy.

What telesis SHOULD provide is a clean handoff interface: when the upstream conversation converges on defined work, there's a smooth way to package it into intake items. Think "loading dock" — telesis doesn't drive the truck, but the dock should be easy to back into.

title: telesis adopt: unified onboarding replacing init + upgrade
body:
The current onboarding story has gaps: `telesis init` assumes greenfield, `telesis upgrade` is narrow (scaffold artifacts only), and the two names (`upgrade` vs `update`) are easily confused.

The evolution is `telesis adopt` — a single command/flow that handles:
- First-time integration into an existing project (with or without existing docs)
- Migration from older telesis version to newer (current `upgrade` functionality)
- Accepting existing docs (PRD, ARCHITECTURE, VISION, MILESTONES) and identifying gaps
- Assisting in creating missing docs rather than demanding the full set upfront

This is the highest-leverage next feature because it unblocks using telesis on real, existing projects — which is where most of the value lies. Greenfield projects are the easy case; the hard case (and common case) is retrofitting a project with years of history.

Design implications:
- `init` may become a special case of `adopt` (greenfield = adopt with no pre-existing state)
- `adopt` should be conversational when driven through Claude + MCP, not a CLI form
- Multiple PRDs per project should be supported for multi-initiative or multi-service monorepos
- PRDs should be rotatable (completed initiatives archived, new ones created)

title: Comparative analysis: Anthropic harness design blog posts vs. telesis
body:
Analyzed two Anthropic engineering blog posts on harness design for long-running agents:
1. "Effective Harnesses for Long-Running Agents" — initializer/coder agent pattern with progress files, immutable feature lists, session startup protocols
2. "Harness Design for Long-Running Application Development" — GAN-inspired generator/evaluator separation, three-agent architecture (planner/generator/evaluator), context resets over compaction, sprint contracts, iterative simplification

**What telesis already validates:**
- Structured handoff artifacts across context windows (telesis stores > ad-hoc progress files)
- Immutable acceptance criteria (milestone AC = their "feature list with locked requirements")
- Generator/evaluator separation (telesis review pipeline = their evaluator agent)
- Incremental feature-by-feature execution (dispatch model = their "one feature at a time")
- Pre-implementation contracts (plan approval = their "sprint contracts")

**Gaps telesis should address:**
1. **Multi-session resilience** — context resets with structured handoff, not compaction. Orchestrator needs a clean restart/resume protocol when context fills mid-milestone. The daemon could manage session lifecycle.
2. **Live verification** — evaluator that exercises running applications (Playwright), not just static code review. Goes beyond milestone check toward automated behavioral verification.
3. **Context anxiety detection** — models rush to "finish" as context fills. Orchestrator should monitor context health and force clean checkpoints before limits hit.
4. **Periodic harness simplification** — "re-examine harnesses when new model versions release." As models improve, some orchestration steps become overhead. Need discipline of pruning.
5. **Environment bootstrapping** — init.sh pattern. telesis adopt could generate/validate dev environment setup to reduce "first 5 minutes" waste each session.
6. **Planner as distinct agent** — dedicated agent expanding terse requirements into specs before coding agent starts. Could improve the upstream → intake handoff.

**Meta-insight from article 2:** "The space of effective harness combinations doesn't shrink as models improve — it shifts. Better models enable solving previously impossible problems rather than eliminating orchestration needs." This validates telesis's existence while warning against over-investing in capability compensation vs. process enforcement.

title: Convergence: daemon as session lifecycle manager — the next architectural evolution
body:
Three independent threads from today's strategic conversation all converge on the same architectural change: the daemon must evolve from "background notifier" to "session lifecycle manager."

**Thread 1 — telesis adopt:** Onboarding an existing project is conversational and may span multiple sessions. The daemon could drive adoption state, persisting progress across sessions as the user incrementally brings a project into telesis.

**Thread 2 — Multi-session resilience:** Anthropic's harness blog posts recommend context resets over compaction for long-running tasks. The orchestrator currently assumes a single continuous session. The daemon should manage orchestrator checkpoints and session restarts — knowing when context is filling, forcing clean checkpoints, and orienting a fresh session from structured state in .telesis/.

**Thread 3 — Provider-neutral enforcement:** Skills and hooks are Claude Code-specific. The daemon is the only component that can provide equivalent enforcement (preflight gating, contextual guidance, process nudging) for non-Claude agents. This makes telesis's provider agnosticism real rather than aspirational.

**The architectural shift:** The daemon becomes the persistent process that outlives any single agent session, holds orchestrator state, and manages handoff between sessions regardless of which LLM provider is driving. This is the daemon's original intended destiny — the walking skeleton made the full picture visible.

**Key design principle from the blog posts:** "Find the simplest solution possible, and only increase complexity when needed." The daemon should grow incrementally, not all at once. But this is the north star for the next major evolution.
